label: Shin contusion without fracture or neurovascular red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a shin bruise.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a shin bruise. We did not find signs of fracture, infection, or poor blood flow today.
home: Use ice wrapped in a cloth for short periods.
Raise the leg when swelling is present.
meds: Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
Use medicines only as prescribed or recommended.
return: New numbness, blue or pale toes, inability to walk, or severe worsening pain.
Spreading redness, fever, pus, or a new wound.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, orthopedics, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated shin contusion.
No fracture concern, open wound, infection, or neurovascular compromise.
exclusion: Fracture or dislocation concern, open wound, infection, neurovascular compromise, inability to bear weight, high-energy trauma, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus bruise material supports contusion self-care, expected symptom course, and escalation for worsening pain, swelling, or concerning symptoms. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed shin contusion with fracture, wound, infection, and neurovascular red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of shin contusion

label: Heel strain without rupture or diabetic-foot red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a heel strain.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a heel strain. We did not find signs of fracture, tendon rupture, infection, poor circulation, or a diabetic-foot emergency today.
home: Use ice wrapped in a cloth for short periods.
Return to activity gradually as pain improves.
meds: Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
Use medicines only as prescribed or recommended.
return: New numbness, blue or pale toes, inability to walk, or severe worsening pain.
Fever, spreading redness, major swelling, or a new wound.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, podiatry, orthopedics, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated heel strain.
No fracture concern, tendon rupture concern, infection, diabetic-foot pathway, or neurovascular compromise.
exclusion: Fracture or dislocation concern, tendon rupture concern, open wound, infection, diabetic foot, peripheral vascular disease, neuropathy, inability to bear weight, high-energy trauma, or specialist-directed podiatry plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus sprain and strain material supports rest, symptom care, gradual return, and escalation for worsening pain, swelling, numbness, or inability to move. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed heel strain with rupture, fracture, diabetic-foot, infection, and neurovascular red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of heel strain

label: Localized mosquito bites without infection or anaphylaxis
summary: Your symptoms fit a local skin reaction from mosquito bites.
found: Your clinician diagnosed localized mosquito bites. We did not find signs of anaphylaxis, serious allergic reaction, spreading infection, or a bite emergency today.
home: Wash the area gently with soap and water.
Use cool compresses for itching or swelling.
meds: Use itch or allergy medicines only if prescribed or recommended.
Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
return: Trouble breathing, throat swelling, vomiting after a bite, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Spreading redness, pus, fever, red streaks, or worsening warmth.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized mosquito bites.
No anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, secondary infection, necrotic spider bite, tick-borne illness concern, or snakebite concern.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, secondary infection, necrotic spider or scorpion concern, tick-borne illness concern, snakebite concern, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed allergy plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus insect bite material supports local bite care, itch control, and escalation for allergic reactions, infection, or systemic symptoms. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized mosquito bites with anaphylaxis and infection red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of localized mosquito bites

label: Localized poison ivy dermatitis without infection or anaphylaxis
summary: Your symptoms fit localized poison ivy dermatitis.
found: Your clinician diagnosed localized poison ivy dermatitis. We did not find signs of anaphylaxis, severe skin reaction, eye or genital involvement, or spreading infection today.
home: Wash the area gently with soap and water.
Wash clothing or objects that may still have plant oil on them.
meds: Use skin or itch medicines only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid or antibiotic creams unless your clinician said they are safe.
return: Trouble breathing, throat swelling, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Fever, spreading redness, pus, red streaks, or worsening warmth.
Rash near the eyes or genitals, skin peeling, mouth sores, or symptoms that worsen.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized poison ivy dermatitis.
No anaphylaxis, infection, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, eye/genital location, burn or chemical wound, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or immunocompromised host.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, infection concern, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, eye or genital rash location, burn or chemical wound, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.

terms: clinician diagnosis localized poison ivy dermatitis

high_confidence_terms: clinician diagnosis localized poison ivy dermatitis
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus contact dermatitis material supports irritant/allergic rash framing, trigger avoidance, washing exposed skin and objects, itch care, and escalation for infection, severe reaction, or concerning symptoms. This expansion is limited to localized poison ivy dermatitis without systemic allergic reaction, infection, eye/genital involvement, or severe cutaneous reaction.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of localized poison ivy dermatitis

label: Dry cracked hands without infection or systemic skin disease
summary: Your symptoms fit dry, cracked skin on the hands.
found: Your clinician diagnosed dry, cracked hands. We did not find signs of spreading infection, deep wound, severe allergic reaction, or a systemic skin emergency today.
home: Avoid harsh soaps or irritating chemicals when you can.
Protect the hands from repeated wet work or cold exposure when possible.
meds: Use skin medicines only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover antibiotic or steroid creams unless your clinician said they are safe.
return: Spreading redness, pus, fever, red streaks, or worsening warmth.
Skin peeling, mouth sores, target-like rash, or feeling very ill.
Painful cracks that deepen, bleed heavily, or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of dry cracked hands or hand xerosis.
No secondary infection, deep wound, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
exclusion: Secondary infection, open deep wound, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, mucosal lesions, systemic illness, immunocompromised host, diabetes with hand wound concern, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus dry skin material supports moisturizer use, avoiding irritants, and seeking care for cracks, bleeding, infection signs, or persistent symptoms. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized dry cracked hands without infection or systemic skin disease.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of dry cracked hands or hand xerosis
